AS! Case-mix trajectories

Ryan Stanley Falck

2026-05-13

  • 1) Data Set-Up and Management
  • 2) Fitting the primary model
    • 2.1) Final model
      • 2.2) Describe classes
  • 3) Moderation analysis
    • 3.1) Moderation by group
    • 3.2) Moderation by age
    • 3.3) Moderation by Sex
    • 3.4) Moderation by cognitive function
    • 3.4) Moderation by physical function
  • 4) Sensitivity Analyses
    • 4.1) Model fit comparisons
      • 4.1.1) Normalized data
      • 4.1.2) Log-transformed data
      • 4.1.3) Final Model
      • 4.1.4) Class descriptions
  • 5) Figure 1 REVISED
  • 6) Figure 2 REVISED

1) Data Set-Up and Management

Load packages

if (!require("pacman")) install.packages("pacman")
## Loading required package: pacman
pacman::p_load(readxl,psych,ggplot2,tableone,lavaan, semPlot, sem, semTools, tidyverse, haven, gbmt, dplyr, lcmm)
## also installing the dependency 'quadprog'
## 
## The downloaded binary packages are in
##  /var/folders/5m/pmdmqbr90qq5_67dpj613jsw0000gp/T//Rtmp6SWuLS/downloaded_packages
## 
## lavaan installed
## also installing the dependencies 'arm', 'RUnit', 'glasso', 'fdrtool', 'mi', 'kutils', 'RcppParallel', 'StanHeaders', 'BH', 'rpf', 'qgraph', 'sem', 'XML', 'igraph', 'lisrelToR', 'rockchalk', 'corpcor', 'OpenMx'
## 
## The downloaded binary packages are in
##  /var/folders/5m/pmdmqbr90qq5_67dpj613jsw0000gp/T//Rtmp6SWuLS/downloaded_packages
## 
## semPlot installed
## 
## The downloaded binary packages are in
##  /var/folders/5m/pmdmqbr90qq5_67dpj613jsw0000gp/T//Rtmp6SWuLS/downloaded_packages
## 
## semTools installed
## 
## The downloaded binary packages are in
##  /var/folders/5m/pmdmqbr90qq5_67dpj613jsw0000gp/T//Rtmp6SWuLS/downloaded_packages
## 
## gbmt installed
## also installing the dependencies 'spacefillr', 'marqLevAlg'
## 
## The downloaded binary packages are in
##  /var/folders/5m/pmdmqbr90qq5_67dpj613jsw0000gp/T//Rtmp6SWuLS/downloaded_packages
## 
## lcmm installed

Set working directory

setwd("~/Desktop/Manuscripts/Ongoing Projects/AS! Case-mix trajectories")

Load data

falls_plus_HRU<-read_excel("AS_Econ_Falls_HRU_merge_Feb 22 2021.xlsx")
geris<-read_excel("AS Geri_July 30 2018.xlsx")
HRUs1 <- read_excel("AS! HRU Q1-3_June 13 2019.xlsx", sheet = "HRU Q1")
HRUs2 <- read_excel("AS! HRU Q1-3_June 13 2019.xlsx", sheet = "HRU Q2")
## Warning: Expecting numeric in B1537 / R1537C2: got 'Lagime'
## Warning: Coercing text to numeric in A4457 / R4457C1: '151'
## Warning: Coercing text to numeric in B4457 / R4457C2: '11'
HRUs3 <- read_excel("AS! HRU Q1-3_June 13 2019.xlsx", sheet = "HRU Q3")

Data management of individual data frames. Preparing for merging

falls_plus_HRU_2<-subset(falls_plus_HRU, assessmenperiod == 1)
falls_plus_HRU_2$Stroop3_2<-as.numeric(falls_plus_HRU_2$`Stroop 3`) - as.numeric(falls_plus_HRU_2$`Stroop 2`)
falls_plus_HRU_2$DSFB<-as.numeric(falls_plus_HRU_2$`Digit F`) - as.numeric(falls_plus_HRU_2$`Digit B`)
falls_plus_HRU_3<-falls_plus_HRU_2[c(1,3,4,7:10,34:36,39,40,41,45,49:51,52:54,65:71,72,76,79,82,83)]
falls_plus_HRU_3<-rename(falls_plus_HRU_3,c("ID"="id"))

geris_2<-subset(geris, ID != 330)

HRUQ1 Management

#Create dataframe
HRUs1_2 <- HRUs1[c(1,2,7)]
HRUs1_3<-rename(HRUs1_2,c("Time"="Assessmen Period"))
HRUs1_4<-subset(HRUs1_3,Time!="Lagime")
HRUs1_4$Time<-as.numeric(HRUs1_4$Time)

#Delete duplicates
HRUs1_5<-distinct(HRUs1_4)

#Resolve remaining multiple values
HRUs1_6<-HRUs1_5[-c(909,3128,3129),] #Resolves double input for ID=197 Time=1. Removes both inputs for ID=369 Time=3 (inputting manually)

#Insert missing values 
ID<-c(171,171,171,251,255,255,255,255,255,369,369)
Time<-c(2,11,12,13,7,8,9,10,11,5,3)
hruq1t<-c(NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,563.3191)
missing<-data.frame(ID,Time,hruq1t)
HRUs1_final<-rbind(HRUs1_6, missing)

HRUQ2 Management

#Create dataframe
HRUs2_2<-rename(HRUs2,c("Time"="Assessmen Period"))
HRUs2_3<-subset(HRUs2_2,Time!="Lagime")
HRUs2_3$Time<-as.numeric(HRUs2_3$Time)

#Delete duplicates
HRUs2_4<-distinct(HRUs2_3)

#No remaining multiple values

#Insert missing values 
ID<-c(171,171,171,251,255,255,255,255,255,369)
Time<-c(2,11,12,13,7,8,9,10,11,5)
hruq2total<-c(NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA)
missing_2<-data.frame(ID,Time,hruq2total)
HRUs2_final<-rbind(HRUs2_4, missing_2)

HRUQ3 Management

HRUs3_2<-rename(HRUs3,c("Time"="Assessmen Period"))
HRUs3_2$Time<-as.factor(HRUs3_2$Time)

#Delete duplicates
HRUs3_3<-distinct(HRUs3_2)

#Resolve remaining multiple values
HRUs3_4<-HRUs3_3[!(HRUs3_3$ID=="369" & HRUs3_3$Time=="3"),]#Removes both inputs for ID=369 Time=3 (inputting manually)

ID<-c(171,171,171,251,255,255,255,255,255,369,369)
Time<-c(2,11,12,13,7,8,9,10,11,5,3)
hruq3total<-c(NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,NA,314.7633)
missing_3<-data.frame(ID,Time,hruq3total)
HRUs3_final<-rbind(HRUs3_4, missing_3)

Merge HRU data

HRUstotal_1<- merge(HRUs1_final,HRUs2_final)
HRUstotal_final<- merge(HRUstotal_1,HRUs3_final)

Create HRU Total Cost

#HRU total cost across three categories
HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost<-HRUstotal_final$hruq1t + HRUstotal_final$hruq2total + HRUstotal_final$hruq3total

#Accounting for inflation
HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost_inflated<-HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost *1.1110

#Because the costs are large numbers, this will shrink the dependent variable and hopefully improve model fit
HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost_small <- HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost_inflated/1000

#As another alternative I normalized cost data in order to account for the issues of non-normality using a min-max normalization
HRUstotal_final$HRU_normalized <- (HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost_small - min(HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost_small, na.rm=TRUE)) / (max(HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost_small, na.rm = TRUE) - min(HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost_small, na.rm = TRUE))

#I also log-transformed cost data in order to account for the issues of non-normality using a min-max normalization
HRUstotal_final$HRU_log <- log(HRUstotal_final$HRU_totalcost_inflated + 1)

Create final data frame for all variables

long.data<-merge(falls_plus_HRU_3, HRUstotal_final)

Final Data Management

long.data2<-long.data[c(1:3,8,33,38:41)]
long.data2$Group<-as.factor(long.data2$Group)

Note: Two participants did not provide HRU data (ID= 330 and ID=450). All other participants were included in the model.

2) Fitting the primary model

This can be done using the lcmm package. We first explored how to fit the data according to the link function and number of polynomials (up to 3 orders). We examined: 1) a linear link function; 2) a spline function using 5 equidistant splines; and 3) a spline function using 3 quantiles. We do not include covariates because this causes issues with the Hessian (i.e., the estimate of the curvature of the log-likelihood surface at the maximum likelihood estimate) - this is likely due to complete separation wherein one or more parameters perfectly predict the response parameter

model1<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~ Time, subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = 'linear', data = long.data2)
model2<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~Time, subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = '3-equi-splines', data = long.data2)
model3<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~Time, subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2)
model4<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~ Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = 'linear', data = long.data2)
model5<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = '3-equi-splines', data = long.data2)
model6<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2)
model7<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2) + I(Time^3), random=~ Time + I(Time^2) + I(Time^3), subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = 'linear', data = long.data2)
model8<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2) + I(Time^3), random=~Time + I(Time^2) + I(Time^3), subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = '3-equi-splines', data = long.data2)
model9<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2) + I(Time^3), random=~Time + I(Time^2) + I(Time^3), subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2)

summarytable(model1, model2, model3, model4, model5, model6, model7, model8, model9, which = c("G", "loglik", "conv", "npm", "AIC", "BIC", "SABIC", "entropy","ICL", "%class"))
##        G     loglik conv npm       AIC       BIC     SABIC entropy      ICL1
## model1 1  -8665.680    1   6 17343.359 17366.386 17347.352       1 17366.386
## model2 1  -4992.355    1   9 10002.709 10037.249 10008.699       1 10037.249
## model3 1  -1165.166    1   9  2348.332  2382.871  2354.321       1  2382.871
## model4 1  -8626.885    1  10 17273.769 17312.146 17280.424       1 17312.146
## model5 1  -4980.610    1  13  9987.221 10037.111  9995.872       1 10037.111
## model6 1  -1150.680    1  13  2327.360  2377.250  2336.011       1  2377.250
## model7 1 -12789.194    2  15 25608.388 25665.954 25618.370       1 25665.954
## model8 1 -11957.565    2  18 23951.130 24020.209 23963.108       1 24020.209
## model9 1  -7320.979    2  18 14677.958 14747.037 14689.937       1 14747.037
##             ICL2 %class1
## model1 17366.386     100
## model2 10037.249     100
## model3  2382.871     100
## model4 17312.146     100
## model5 10037.111     100
## model6  2377.250     100
## model7 25665.954     100
## model8 24020.209     100
## model9 14747.037     100

We then compared the estimated functions by plot.

col <- rainbow(10)
plot(model1, which="linkfunction",bty='l', ylab="HRUCost", col=col[1], lwd=2, xlab="underlying latent process")
plot(model2, which="linkfunction", add=TRUE, col=col[2], lwd=2)
plot(model3, which="linkfunction", add=TRUE, col=col[3], lwd=2)
plot(model4, which="linkfunction", add=TRUE, col=col[4], lwd=2)
plot(model5, which="linkfunction", add=TRUE, col=col[5], lwd=2)
plot(model6, which="linkfunction", add=TRUE, col=col[6], lwd=2)
plot(model7, which="linkfunction", add=TRUE, col=col[7], lwd=2)
plot(model8, which="linkfunction", add=TRUE, col=col[8], lwd=2)
plot(model9, which="linkfunction", add=TRUE, col=col[9], lwd=2)
legend(x="topleft", legend=c("model1", "model2","model3","model4","model5","model6","model7","model8","model9"), lty=1, col=col, bty="n", lwd=2)

The results suggest that either a 2nd or 3rd order model using 3 splines at 25%, 50% and 75% provides the best fit. Next we investigate the number of groups in each model.

First order models are described below. The best fitting models is model3d (4 classes).

model3a<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~Time, subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2)
model3b<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~Time, mixture =~ Time, subject='ID', ng = 2 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model3)
model3c<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~Time, mixture =~ Time, subject='ID', ng = 3 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model3)
model3d<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~Time, mixture =~ Time, subject='ID', ng = 4 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model3)
model3e<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~Time, mixture =~ Time, subject='ID', ng = 5 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model3)
model3f<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time, random=~Time, mixture =~ Time, subject='ID', ng = 6 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model3)

summarytable(model3a, model3b, model3c, model3d, model3e, model3f, which = c("G", "loglik", "conv", "npm", "AIC", "BIC", "SABIC", "entropy","ICL", "%class"))
##         G    loglik conv npm      AIC      BIC    SABIC   entropy     ICL1
## model3a 1 -1165.166    1   9 2348.332 2382.871 2354.321 1.0000000 2382.871
## model3b 2 -1154.387    2  12 2332.773 2378.826 2340.759 0.9228055 2397.179
## model3c 3 -1154.385    2  15 2338.770 2396.336 2348.753 0.4764691 2593.615
## model3d 4 -1134.473    2  18 2304.946 2374.025 2316.925 0.7875196 2475.059
## model3e 5 -1134.459    2  21 2310.919 2391.511 2324.894 0.5872627 2619.358
## model3f 6 -1129.362    2  24 2306.724 2398.829 2322.695 0.6685347 2602.539
##             ICL2     %class1  %class2   %class3    %class4  %class5 %class6
## model3a 2382.871 100.0000000                                               
## model3b 2393.879   2.9154519 97.08455                                      
## model3c 2588.697   3.2069971 96.79300  0.000000                            
## model3d 2474.652   0.8746356 71.42857  2.623907 25.0728863                 
## model3e 2628.907   0.8746356 29.44606 67.055394  2.6239067  0.00000        
## model3f 2595.671   2.3323615 64.72303  2.332362  0.8746356 29.73761       0

Second order models are described below. Model6c (3 classes), model6d (4 classes), model6e (5 classes), and model6f (6 classes) are the best fitting models

model6a<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 1 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2)
model6b<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~Time + I(Time^2), mixture =~ Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 2 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model6)
model6c<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~Time + I(Time^2), mixture =~ Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 3 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model6)
model6d<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~Time + I(Time^2), mixture =~ Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 4 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model6)
model6e<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~Time + I(Time^2), mixture =~ Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 5 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model6)
model6f<-lcmm(HRU_totalcost_small~Time + I(Time^2), random=~Time + I(Time^2), mixture =~ Time + I(Time^2), subject='ID', ng = 6 , link = '3-quant-splines', data = long.data2, B=model6)

summarytable(model6a, model6b, model6c, model6d, model6e, model6f, which = c("G", "loglik", "conv", "npm", "AIC", "BIC", "SABIC", "entropy","ICL", "%class"))
##         G    loglik conv npm      AIC      BIC    SABIC   entropy     ICL1
## model6a 1 -1150.680    1  13 2327.360 2377.250 2336.011 1.0000000 2377.250
## model6b 2 -1088.553    1  17 2211.107 2276.348 2222.420 0.9993027 2276.514
## model6c 3 -1062.679    2  21 2167.357 2247.950 2181.333 0.9967722 2249.166
## model6d 4 -1079.445    2  25 2208.890 2304.833 2225.527 0.7239712 2436.085
## model6e 5 -1058.754    2  29 2175.508 2286.802 2194.807 0.6715704 2468.107
## model6f 6 -1048.670    2  33 2163.341 2289.986 2185.302 0.8372402 2390.014
##             ICL2    %class1   %class2    %class3    %class4   %class5  %class6
## model6a 2377.250 100.000000                                                   
## model6b 2276.413   1.457726 98.542274                                         
## model6c 2248.936   1.457726 97.667638  0.8746356                              
## model6d 2430.642   1.457726 70.845481 23.9067055  3.7900875                   
## model6e 2455.190   1.457726  1.457726 94.1690962  0.8746356 2.0408163         
## model6f 2387.058   1.166181  1.457726 67.0553936 27.1137026 0.8746356 2.332362

The best fitting models are then compared here

summarytable(model3d, model6c, model6d, model6e, model6f, which = c("G", "loglik", "conv", "npm", "AIC", "BIC", "SABIC", "entropy","ICL", "%class"))
##         G    loglik conv npm      AIC      BIC    SABIC   entropy     ICL1
## model6c 3 -1062.679    2  21 2167.357 2247.950 2181.333 0.9967722 2249.166
## model3d 4 -1134.473    2  18 2304.946 2374.025 2316.925 0.7875196 2475.059
## model6d 4 -1079.445    2  25 2208.890 2304.833 2225.527 0.7239712 2436.085
## model6e 5 -1058.754    2  29 2175.508 2286.802 2194.807 0.6715704 2468.107
## model6f 6 -1048.670    2  33 2163.341 2289.986 2185.302 0.8372402 2390.014
##             ICL2   %class1   %class2    %class3    %class4   %class5  %class6
## model6c 2248.936 1.4577259 97.667638  0.8746356                              
## model3d 2474.652 0.8746356 71.428571  2.6239067 25.0728863                   
## model6d 2430.642 1.4577259 70.845481 23.9067055  3.7900875                   
## model6e 2455.190 1.4577259  1.457726 94.1690962  0.8746356 2.0408163         
## model6f 2387.058 1.1661808  1.457726 67.0553936 27.1137026 0.8746356 2.332362

Summary plots comparing models are here

summaryplot(model3d, model6c, model6d, model6e, model6f, which = c("AIC", "BIC", "entropy", "ICL"))

Based on these results, it seems that each model is somewhat comparable. Thus, we plotted each of the case-mix trajectories in order to determine which one appeared to best explain the data

Model 3d: 1st order with 4 classes

OEP_data<-data.frame(Time= seq(1,13, length=1000))
OEP_pred <- predictY(model3d, OEP_data, var.time ="Time", draws = FALSE)
plot(OEP_pred, col=c("red","navy", "green", "purple"), lwd = 2, xlab="Time (Months)", ylab="HRU Total Cost (per $1000)", legend=NULL, main="Linear Model: 4 Classes",ylim=c(0,5))
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("Class 1","Class 2", "Class 3", "Class 4"), col=c("red","navy", "green",  "purple"), lty=1,lwd=2, ncol=1, bty="n", cex = 0.7)

Model 6C: 2nd order with 3 classes

OEP_data<-data.frame(Time= seq(1,13, length=1000))
OEP_pred <- predictY(model6c, OEP_data, var.time ="Time", draws = FALSE)
plot(OEP_pred, col=c("red","navy", "green"), lwd = 2, xlab="Time (Months)", ylab="HRU Total Cost (per $1000)", legend=NULL,  main="Quadratic Model: 3 Classes",ylim=c(0,10))
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("Class 1","Class 2", "Class 3"), col=c("red","navy", "green"), lty=1,lwd=2, ncol=1, bty="n", cex = 0.7)

Model 6D: 2nd order with 4 classes

OEP_data<-data.frame(Time= seq(1,13, length=1000))
OEP_pred <- predictY(model6d, OEP_data, var.time ="Time", draws = FALSE)
plot(OEP_pred, col=c("red","navy", "green", "purple"), lwd = 2, xlab="Time (Months)", ylab="HRU Total Cost (per $1000)",legend=NULL,main="Quadratic Model: 4 Classes",ylim=c(0,10))
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("Class 1","Class 2", "Class 3", "Class 4"), col=c("red","navy", "green", "purple"), lty=1,lwd=2, ncol=1, bty="n", cex = 0.7)

Model 6E: 2nd order with 5 classes

OEP_data<-data.frame(Time= seq(1,13, length=1000))
OEP_pred <- predictY(model6e, OEP_data, var.time ="Time", draws = FALSE)
plot(OEP_pred, col=c("red","navy", "green", "purple", "gold"), lwd = 2, xlab="Time (Months)", ylab="HRU Total Cost (per$1000)",legend=NULL,  main="Quadratic Model: 5 Classes",ylim=c(0,10))
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("Class 1","Class 2", "Class 3", "Class 4", "Class 5"), col=c("red","navy", "green", "purple", "gold"), lty=1,lwd=2, ncol=1, bty="n", cex = 0.7)

Model 6F: 2nd order with 6 classes

OEP_data<-data.frame(Time= seq(1,13, length=1000))
OEP_pred <- predictY(model6f, OEP_data, var.time ="Time", draws = FALSE)
plot(OEP_pred, col=c("red","navy", "green", "purple", "gold", "magenta"), lwd = 2, xlab="Time (Months)", ylab="HRU Total Cost (per$1000)",legend=NULL,  main="Quadratic Model: 6 Classes",ylim=c(0,10))
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("Class 1","Class 2", "Class 3", "Class 4", "Class 5", "Class 6"), col=c("red","navy", "green", "purple", "gold", "magenta"), lty=1,lwd=2, ncol=1, bty="n", cex = 0.7)

2.1) Final model

Based on these data, the final model was a linear 4 class model using 3-quant splines.

summary(model3d)
## General latent class mixed model 
##      fitted by maximum likelihood method 
##  
## lcmm(fixed = HRU_totalcost_small ~ Time, mixture = ~Time, random = ~Time, 
##     subject = "ID", ng = 4, link = "3-quant-splines", data = long.data2)
##  
## Statistical Model: 
##      Dataset: long.data2 
##      Number of subjects: 343 
##      Number of observations: 4445 
##      Number of observations deleted: 14 
##      Number of latent classes: 4 
##      Number of parameters: 18  
##      Link function: Quadratic I-splines with nodes  
## 0 0.1251695 50.315  
##  
## Iteration process: 
##      Maximum number of iteration reached without convergence 
##      Number of iterations:  100 
##      Convergence criteria: parameters= 8.2e-06 
##                          : likelihood= 0.00048 
##                          : second derivatives= 1 
##  
## Goodness-of-fit statistics: 
##      maximum log-likelihood: -1134.47  
##      AIC: 2304.95  
##      BIC: 2374.03  
##  
##  
## Maximum Likelihood Estimates: 
##  
## Fixed effects in the class-membership model:
## (the class of reference is the last class) 
## 
##                      coef Se Wald p-value
## intercept class1 -3.24276                
## intercept class2  0.87003                
## intercept class3 -2.43013                
## 
## Fixed effects in the longitudinal model:
## 
##                                      coef Se Wald p-value
## intercept class1 (not estimated)        0                
## intercept class2                 -2.08050                
## intercept class3                 -2.29040                
## intercept class4                 -2.30242                
## Time class1                      -0.29295                
## Time class2                      -0.00497                
## Time class3                       0.19142                
## Time class4                      -0.06726                
## 
## 
## Variance-covariance matrix of the random-effects:
##           intercept    Time
## intercept   0.08933        
## Time       -0.00365 0.00015
## 
## Residual standard error (not estimated) = 1
## 
## Parameters of the link function:
## 
##                coef Se Wald p-value
## I-splines1 -3.36655                
## I-splines2  1.18471                
## I-splines3  3.53339                
## I-splines4 -0.00031                
## I-splines5  0.22253
OEP_data<-data.frame(Time= seq(1,13, length=1000))
OEP_pred <- predictY(model3d, OEP_data, var.time ="Time", draws = FALSE)
plot(OEP_pred, col=c("red","navy", "green", "purple"), lwd = 2, xlab="Time (Months)", ylab="HRU Total Cost (per $1000)", legend=NULL, main="Case-mix trajectories for Health Resource Utilization (2021 CAD)",ylim=c(0,5))
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("Class 1","Class 2", "Class 3", "Class 4"), col=c("red","navy", "green",  "purple"), lty=1,lwd=2, ncol=1, bty="n", cex = 0.7)

Plot in ggplot2

OEP_pred <- predictY(model3d, OEP_data, var.time ="Time", draws = FALSE)
Time<-OEP_pred$times
Classes<-as.data.frame(OEP_pred$pred)
OEP_data_frame<-cbind(Time, Classes)

Class1_frame<-OEP_data_frame[c(1,2)]
Class2_frame<-OEP_data_frame[c(1,3)]
Class3_frame<-OEP_data_frame[c(1,4)]
Class4_frame<-OEP_data_frame[c(1,5)]
Class1_frame$Class<- "Class 1"
Class2_frame$Class<- "Class 2"
Class3_frame$Class<- "Class 3"
Class4_frame$Class<- "Class 4"
Class1_frame <- rename(Class1_frame, "Predicted" = "Ypred_class1")
Class2_frame <- rename(Class2_frame, "Predicted" = "Ypred_class2")
Class3_frame <- rename(Class3_frame, "Predicted" = "Ypred_class3")
Class4_frame <- rename(Class4_frame, "Predicted" = "Ypred_class4")

graph_data<-rbind(Class1_frame, Class2_frame, Class3_frame, Class4_frame)

figure1<-ggplot(graph_data, aes(x=Time, y=Predicted, group=Class)) + geom_smooth(aes(colour=Class)) + theme_classic() + xlab("Time (Months)") + ylab("HRU Total Cost (per $1000 CAD") + ylim(0,5)

ggsave("Figure 1 - case trajectories.tiff",figure1, width = 4, height = 4, dpi = 300)
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'gam' and formula = 'y ~ s(x, bs = "cs")'
## Warning: Removed 163 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_smooth()`).

Predicted posterior probabilities

postprob(model3d)
##  
## Posterior classification: 
##   class1 class2 class3 class4
## N   3.00 245.00   9.00  86.00
## %   0.87  71.43   2.62  25.07
##  
## Posterior classification table: 
##      --> mean of posterior probabilities in each class 
##         prob1  prob2  prob3  prob4
## class1 0.9501 0.0396 0.0000 0.0103
## class2 0.0018 0.8903 0.0013 0.1066
## class3 0.0000 0.0811 0.9189 0.0000
## class4 0.0060 0.1629 0.0000 0.8311
##  
## Posterior probabilities above a threshold (%): 
##          class1 class2 class3 class4
## prob>0.7 100.00  88.57  88.89  73.26
## prob>0.8 100.00  79.59  88.89  63.95
## prob>0.9  66.67  64.90  77.78  51.16
##

2.2) Describe classes

Create data frame with classes

model3d.frame<-model3d$pprob

baseline.data<-merge(model3d.frame, falls_plus_HRU_3, by="ID")

baseline.data.2 <- rename(baseline.data, "Falls_number" = "Total Falls", "Final_Weight" = "Final Weight", "Final_Height"="Final Height", "WHR"="Waist to Hip Ratio","FCI"="FCI Total",
                          "IADL"="Total IADL", "TrailsBA"="Trail Difference","TUG"="Mean TUG", "GaitSpeed"="Gait Speed", "SPPB"="NIA Total Score") 

baseline.data.2$Gender[baseline.data.2$Gender==1]<-"Female"
baseline.data.2$Gender[baseline.data.2$Gender==2]<-"Male"

baseline.data.2$BMI<-baseline.data.2$Final_Weight/((baseline.data.2$Final_Height/100)^2)

Describe participants

baseline.vars<-dput(names(baseline.data.2[c(8,13,7,17,16,38,20,21,23,12,33,34,35,24,25,36,37)]))
## c("Group", "Age", "Gender", "Education", "WHR", "BMI", "GDS", 
## "FCI", "IADL", "Falls_number", "TUG", "GaitSpeed", "SPPB", "MMSE", 
## "MoCA", "Stroop3_2", "DSFB")
baseline.data.2$Group<-as.factor(baseline.data.2$Group)

Table1_full<-CreateTableOne(vars=baseline.vars, data=baseline.data.2)
print(Table1_full,contDigits=2,missing=TRUE,quote=TRUE)
##                                                      ""
##  ""                                                   "Overall"       "Missing"
##   "n"                                                 "  343"         "    "   
##   "Group = 1 (%)"                                     "  172 (50.1) " " 0.0"   
##   "Age (mean (SD))"                                   "81.58 (6.09)"  " 0.0"   
##   "Gender = Male (%)"                                 "  115 (33.5) " " 0.0"   
##   "Education (%)"                                     "  "            "20.1"   
##   "   Grades 9-13, without certificate or diploma"    "   22 ( 8.0) " "    "   
##   "   High school certificate or diploma"             "   47 (17.2) " "    "   
##   "   Less than grade 9"                              "    8 ( 2.9) " "    "   
##   "   Some university without certificate or diploma" "   26 ( 9.5) " "    "   
##   "   Trades or professional certificate or diploma"  "   41 (15.0) " "    "   
##   "   University certificate or diploma"              "   13 ( 4.7) " "    "   
##   "   University degree"                              "  117 (42.7) " "    "   
##   "WHR (mean (SD))"                                   " 0.90 (0.10)"  "14.9"   
##   "BMI (mean (SD))"                                   "27.22 (6.41)"  " 0.6"   
##   "GDS (mean (SD))"                                   " 2.87 (2.52)"  " 0.0"   
##   "FCI (mean (SD))"                                   " 3.78 (1.99)"  " 0.6"   
##   "IADL (mean (SD))"                                  " 7.30 (1.17)"  " 0.3"   
##   "Falls_number (mean (SD))"                          " 1.75 (3.03)"  " 0.0"   
##   "TUG (mean (SD))"                                   "16.61 (6.71)"  " 0.3"   
##   "GaitSpeed (mean (SD))"                             " 0.77 (0.34)"  " 0.0"   
##   "SPPB (mean (SD))"                                  " 7.87 (2.25)"  " 0.0"   
##   "MMSE (mean (SD))"                                  "27.76 (1.63)"  " 0.0"   
##   "MoCA (mean (SD))"                                  "23.15 (3.36)"  " 0.0"   
##   "Stroop3_2 (mean (SD))"                             "77.96 (41.70)" " 1.5"   
##   "DSFB (mean (SD))"                                  " 3.58 (1.91)"  " 2.0"
Table1_stratified<-CreateTableOne(vars=baseline.vars, strata="class", data=baseline.data.2)
print(Table1_stratified,contDigits=2,missing=TRUE,quote=TRUE)
